behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: The failure would be that the code ignores the changed-files list and analyzes all files in the repo instead. A Unit test of the analysis function alone won't catch this — the bug is in the integration between 'get changed files' and 'run analysis'. We need to verify that the set of files passed to the linter matches the PR's changed files. This requires setting up multiple files (some changed, some not) and verifying only changed ones are analyzed. GitHub API mocking is acceptable for this test.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock PR context with 10 changed files and 20 unchanged files in the repo. Mock the GitHub API to report those 10 as changed. Run the lint action and verify that the linter is invoked only on the 10 changed files, not all 30.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: The failure could occur in either the grouping logic (Unit-testable) or the posting mechanism (Integration-testable). To catch all real failures — wrong grouping, missing severity sections, malformed comment structure — we need to test the full flow: collect findings, group them, format them, and verify the GitHub API is called with the correct comment body. A Unit test of just the formatting function would miss failures in how the comment is posted or how the groups are ordered.
behaviors.test_description: Generate 10 mock findings with 3 errors, 4 warnings, and 3 info messages. Mock the GitHub API comment endpoint. Run the action and verify the posted comment contains three distinct sections (errors, warnings, info) in the correct order with all findings properly grouped and formatted.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: This is inherently a performance and timing test. Mocking the linter or analysis would hide real slowness — the mock would return instantly, and the test would always pass. Real failure only manifests with actual file I/O, real linter processes, and real analysis time. We must use real files and run the actual linter to measure end-to-end performance. This cannot be safely tested at Integration level without risking a false pass.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with 200 realistic source files (mixed languages, various sizes). Run the actual linter rules on all of them. Measure total wall-clock time from analysis start to results posted. Assert that the full pipeline completes in under 10 minutes.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, remaining files are still analyzed and results are posted.
behaviors.justification: A real failure here is that the action crashes or stops when the linter subprocess crashes on a single file. To catch this, we must trigger a real subprocess crash — mocking the linter won't cause it to crash. We need a real linter attempting to analyze a truly malformed file that causes it to exit with an error. This requires a real filesystem with actual bad input files and a real linter subprocess. We don't need the full GitHub Action environment (System level) — a local temp directory is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with 10 source files: 9 valid, 1 malformed (invalid syntax that crashes the linter). Run the lint action. Verify that despite the crash on file 10, findings from files 1-9 are collected and the PR comment is still posted with those results.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is pure data-transformation logic. The deduplication is a function that takes a list of findings and returns a deduplicated list. We can test this with synthetic test data (findings created in code) without any subprocess, filesystem, or network calls. The logic is deterministic: given the same input, it always produces the same output. A Unit test passes in findings with duplicates and verifies the output contains no duplicates.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of 8 findings: 4 on line 10 (same message, different rule origins), 4 on line 20 (unique). Pass to deduplication function. Verify the output contains 4 unique findings: 1 on line 10, 3 on line 20 (duplicates collapsed).
